what_it_does: This file presents a side-by-side comparison of four architectural options (A-D) for a code/design substrate ledger, scored across five criteria, as part of P11.
observations: Compares four options: A read-only lens, B runtime-reconciler, C parallel+inbox, D vault=canonical dir
Evaluates each against identity law, two-writer conflict, iOS write-back, code to build, and reversibility
Option C is described as the most aligned with mark-is-truth because annotations are additive rather than overwriting
Option D is flagged as a risky shortcut that trades mastership for convenience and requires a §4 check
Contains YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author session, channel, and a part_of link to board://item-80c37615
No code symbols or implementation details are present
standouts: Option B is called the most capable and truest to mark-is-truth at the cost of a real ingest path
Option C is singled out as the conflict-free physical subset most aligned with mark-is-truth
Option D is explicitly framed as a trap that must clear a §4 check before being accepted
concerns: The §4 check is referenced but not defined in this file
The iOS write-back goal is mentioned without context
No explicit weighting or decision criteria are supplied, so the comparison remains qualitative
Option D's risk depends on assumptions about Obsidian/git/file races that are not detailed here
notes: A design-options matrix comparing four persistence/sync strategies for a mark-is-truth ledger, favoring runtime truth or additive inbox over vault-as-canonical.
questions: What is the §4 check referenced for option D?
What is the precise definition of mark-is-truth in this system?
What is the iOS write-back goal that option A fails?
What is P11 and how do these options relate to it?
How were the five evaluation criteria chosen and weighted?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to show the options on three axes side by side without converging; the file does exactly that via a comparison table.
intention_signals: The file seems to be trying to prevent premature convergence on a vault-as-canonical design by making the trade-offs explicit and elevating runtime-reconciler and additive-inbox patterns.
novelty: It reframes the common 'vault is canonical directory' approach as a dangerous shortcut rather than a safe default, and treats an additive annotation inbox as a first-class architectural option.
contribution: This file contributes a structured, criteria-based comparison of four persistence/sync strategies and the design insight that additive inbox annotation preserves mark-is-truth while making the vault canonical can silently transfer mastership away from the runtime.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item presenting a four-option comparison matrix for persistence/sync design choices in a mark-is-truth ledger, evaluating identity law, conflict, write-back, build cost, and reversibility.
intention_for_embedding: To compare candidate architectural options side by side without selecting a winner, highlighting trade-offs between runtime truth, additive inbox, and vault-as-canonical approaches.
